per_question.question: What information is stored for each customer?
per_question.expected_answer: Each customer has a unique ID, full name, email address, geographic region code, creation date, and active status. The email must be unique across all customers.

`CUSTOMER_MASTER` stores the following customer information: **`FULL_NAME`** (VARCHAR(200), NOT NULL), **`EMAIL`** (VARCHAR(150), NOT NULL, unique for login), **`REGION_CODE`** (VARCHAR(10), nullable; geographic region for tax calculation), **`CREATED_AT`** (DATETIME, NOT NULL; account creation timestamp), and **`IS_ACTIVE`** (BOOLEAN, NOT NULL; whether the customer can place orders). Customer delivery/billing addresses are part of the business concepts, but they are explicitly stated as **not included in the simplified schema**.

# Business Glossary - E-Commerce Domain (Simplified)

This document defines the core business concepts for the e-commerce platform test dataset.

CUSTOMER
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
**Definition**: An individual or entity registered on the platform who has made at least one purchase. **Business Context**: Customers are the primary actors in the e-commerce system. They browse products, place orders, and receive shipments. **Key Attributes**: - Unique Customer ID - Valid email address (required for login) - One or more delivery/billing addresses - Geographic region for tax calculation - Active/inactive status **Business Rules**: - Must place at least one order to remain active - Can have multiple addresses but only one primary per type - Data retained for 7 years after last purchase **Synonyms**: client, buyer, account holder, user **Related Concepts**: CustomerAddress, SalesOrder **Examples**: - A new user registers and places their first order - An existing customer updates their shipping address ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── PRODUCT ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A tangible or digital item offered for sale on the platform. **Business Context**: Products are the core items that customers purchase. They are organized into categories and have inventory tracked across warehouses. **Key Attributes**: - Unique SKU code - Human-readable product name - Unit price in base currency (EUR) - Active/inactive status - Belongs to exactly one Category **Business Rules**: - Must belong to a valid category before being sold - Prices can change daily based on market conditions - Inactive products cannot be ordered but remain for historical reference **Synonyms**: item, article, goods, merchandise, SKU **Related Concepts**: Category, Inventory, OrderLineItem **Examples**: - "Wireless Bluetooth Headphones - Black" (SKU: ELE-001234) - "Cotton T-Shirt - Medium, Blue" (SKU: CLO-002345) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── SALES ORDER ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A formal transaction document recording the agreement between the platform and a Customer to supply Products. **Business Context**: Orders are created when customers complete checkout. They track the customer, items, payment status, and delivery information. **Key Attributes**: - Unique Order ID - References exactly one Customer - Order date when transaction was initiated - Total monetary value - Status: PENDING, CONFIRMED, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED - Must contain at least one Order Line Item **Business Rules**: - Cannot be shipped until payment is confirmed - Can only be cancelled if not yet shipped - Status changes are logged for audit purposes **Synonyms**: order, purchase order, transaction, customer order **Related Concepts**: Customer, OrderLineItem, CustomerAddress **Examples**: - Order #12345 for Customer #10001 containing 3 items - Order #12346 with status "DELIVERED" from last week ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ORDER LINE ITEM ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A single line within a Sales Order specifying a Product, quantity, and unit price at time of purchase. **Business Context**: Line items break down orders into individual product purchases. Each captures the price at the moment of ordering. **Key Attributes**: - Unique line identifier - References exactly one Product - Quantity of units ordered - Unit price at time of order (historical, not current) - Extended amount = quantity × unit price

per_question.question: How are products categorized on the platform?
per_question.expected_answer: Products belong to categories which can have a parent category, forming a hierarchical tree structure. Each product references exactly one category through its CATEGORY_ID.
per_question.generated_answer:
Products are categorized by the category reference stored on each product record in `TB_PRODUCT`.

`TB_PRODUCT` includes `CATEGORY_ID (INT, NOT NULL)` described as the “Category reference.” It is enforced by the foreign key `TB_PRODUCT.CATEGORY_ID -> TB_CATEGORY(CATEGORY_ID)`, so a product “belongs to exactly one Category” and “must belong to a valid category before being sold.”

Categories themselves can form a hierarchy: `TB_CATEGORY` has `PARENT_CATEGORY_ID (INT, nullable)` as a self-reference to `TB_CATEGORY(CATEGORY_ID)`.

per_question.question: What is the relationship between customers and sales orders?
per_question.expected_answer: Each sales order is placed by exactly one customer, referenced through the CUST_ID foreign key. A customer can place zero or more orders over time.
per_question.generated_answer:
Customers and sales orders have a one-to-many relationship: a Customer places zero or more Sales Orders, and each Sales Order is placed by exactly one Customer.

In the schema, this is enforced by the foreign key from the sales order header to the customer master: **SALES_ORDER_HDR.CUST_ID (INT, NOT NULL)** → **CUSTOMER_MASTER.CUST_ID**. Because **SALES_ORDER_HDR.CUST_ID is NOT NULL**, every Sales Order must be associated with a valid Customer. per_question.question: How can I find all orders placed by a specific customer?
per_question.expected_answer: Query the SALES_ORDER_HDR table filtering by CUST_ID. Each order has a foreign key reference to CUSTOMER_MASTER.CUST_ID, so joining these tables on CUST_ID will show all orders for a customer along with their details.

To find all orders placed by a specific customer, use the relationship between `CUSTOMER_MASTER` and `SALES_ORDER_HDR`.

In the schema, `SALES_ORDER_HDR.CUST_ID` is `NOT NULL` and is a foreign key to `CUSTOMER_MASTER.CUST_ID` (“Customer who placed order”), so you can filter orders by the customer’s `CUST_ID`. If you only know the customer’s `EMAIL`, join `CUSTOMER_MASTER` to `SALES_ORDER_HDR` on `CUST_ID`.

If you know `CUSTOMER_MASTER.CUST_ID`:
```sql
SELECT
  so.ORDER_ID,
  so.ORDER_DATE,
  so.TOTAL_AMT,
  so.STATUS_CODE,
  so.PAYMENT_CONFIRMED_AT,
  so.DELIVERED_AT,
  so.CREATED_AT
FROM SALES_ORDER_HDR so
WHERE so.CUST_ID = <customer_cust_id>
ORDER BY so.ORDER_DATE;
```

If you know `CUSTOMER_MASTER.EMAIL`:
```sql
SELECT
  so.ORDER_ID,
  so.ORDER_DATE,
  so.TOTAL_AMT,
  so.STATUS_CODE,
  so.PAYMENT_CONFIRMED_AT,
  so.DELIVERED_AT,
  so.CREATED_AT
FROM CUSTOMER_MASTER cm
JOIN SALES_ORDER_HDR so
  ON so.CUST_ID = cm.CUST_ID
WHERE cm.EMAIL = '<customer_email>'
ORDER BY so.ORDER_DATE;
```

# Business Glossary - E-Commerce Domain (Simplified)

This document defines the core business concepts for the e-commerce platform test dataset.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CUSTOMER
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
**Definition**: An individual or entity registered on the platform who has made at least one purchase. **Business Context**: Customers are the primary actors in the e-commerce system. They browse products, place orders, and receive shipments. **Key Attributes**: - Unique Customer ID - Valid email address (required for login) - One or more delivery/billing addresses - Geographic region for tax calculation - Active/inactive status **Business Rules**: - Must place at least one order to remain active - Can have multiple addresses but only one primary per type - Data retained for 7 years after last purchase **Synonyms**: client, buyer, account holder, user **Related Concepts**: CustomerAddress, SalesOrder **Examples**: - A new user registers and places their first order - An existing customer updates their shipping address ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── PRODUCT ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A tangible or digital item offered for sale on the platform. **Business Context**: Products are the core items that customers purchase. They are organized into categories and have inventory tracked across warehouses. **Key Attributes**: - Unique SKU code - Human-readable product name - Unit price in base currency (EUR) - Active/inactive status - Belongs to exactly one Category **Business Rules**: - Must belong to a valid category before being sold - Prices can change daily based on market conditions - Inactive products cannot be ordered but remain for historical reference **Synonyms**: item, article, goods, merchandise, SKU **Related Concepts**: Category, Inventory, OrderLineItem **Examples**: - "Wireless Bluetooth Headphones - Black" (SKU: ELE-001234) - "Cotton T-Shirt - Medium, Blue" (SKU: CLO-002345) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── SALES ORDER ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A formal transaction document recording the agreement between the platform and a Customer to supply Products. **Business Context**: Orders are created when customers complete checkout. They track the customer, items, payment status, and delivery information. **Key Attributes**: - Unique Order ID - References exactly one Customer - Order date when transaction was initiated - Total monetary value - Status: PENDING, CONFIRMED, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED - Must contain at least one Order Line Item **Business Rules**: - Cannot be shipped until payment is confirmed - Can only be cancelled if not yet shipped - Status changes are logged for audit purposes **Synonyms**: order, purchase order, transaction, customer order **Related Concepts**: Customer, OrderLineItem, CustomerAddress **Examples**: - Order #12345 for Customer #10001 containing 3 items - Order #12346 with status "DELIVERED" from last week ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ORDER LINE ITEM ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A single line within a Sales Order specifying a Product, quantity, and unit price at time of purchase. **Business Context**: Line items break down orders into individual product purchases. Each captures the price at the moment of ordering. **Key Attributes**: - Unique line identifier - References exactly one Product - Quantity of units ordered - Unit price at time of order (historical, not current) - Extended amount = quantity × unit price

per_question.question: How does the schema model the confirmation state of a payment and its relationship to the order?
per_question.expected_answer: Payment confirmation is tracked through two schema fields: PAYMENT.CONFIRMED_AT (nullable DATETIME — NULL means not yet confirmed) and PAYMENT.STATUS_CODE constrained to PENDING, CONFIRMED, FAILED, or REFUNDED. At the order level, SALES_ORDER_HDR.PAYMENT_CONFIRMED_AT is a nullable datetime that mirrors this event. The order STATUS_CODE defaults to PENDING and its lifecycle is defined by the CHECK constraint: PENDING, CONFIRMED, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, or CANCELLED.
per_question.generated_answer:
The schema models a payment’s confirmation state in the **PAYMENT** table using **PAYMENT.STATUS_CODE** and **PAYMENT.CONFIRMED_AT**. **PAYMENT.STATUS_CODE** stores the payment status with the allowed values **PENDING, CONFIRMED, FAILED, REFUNDED**, and **PAYMENT.CONFIRMED_AT** stores the payment confirmation timestamp (**DATETIME**) and is nullable, so it can be missing when the payment is not yet confirmed.

A payment is related to its order through **PAYMENT.ORDER_ID (BIGINT, NOT NULL)**, which has a foreign key to **SALES_ORDER_HDR(ORDER_ID)**. This means each **PAYMENT** record references exactly one **Sales Order**, and you connect a payment to its order by joining **PAYMENT.ORDER_ID = SALES_ORDER_HDR.ORDER_ID**.

At the order level, **SALES_ORDER_HDR** also records an order-level payment confirmation timestamp in **SALES_ORDER_HDR.PAYMENT_CONFIRMED_AT (DATETIME, nullable)**. This aligns with the business rule that **payment must be confirmed before order ships**, by capturing confirmation both on the individual payment record (**PAYMENT.STATUS_CODE / PAYMENT.CONFIRMED_AT**) and summarized on the order header (**SALES_ORDER_HDR.PAYMENT_CONFIRMED_AT**). The business context also supports multiple payment attempts per order (multiple **PAYMENT** rows can reference the same **SALES_ORDER_HDR.ORDER_ID**), and refunds are represented as new payment records with negative amounts.

# Business Glossary - E-Commerce Domain (Simplified)

This document defines the core business concepts for the e-commerce platform test dataset.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CUSTOMER
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
**Definition**: An individual or entity registered on the platform who has made at least one purchase. **Business Context**: Customers are the primary actors in the e-commerce system. They browse products, place orders, and receive shipments. **Key Attributes**: - Unique Customer ID - Valid email address (required for login) - One or more delivery/billing addresses - Geographic region for tax calculation - Active/inactive status **Business Rules**: - Must place at least one order to remain active - Can have multiple addresses but only one primary per type - Data retained for 7 years after last purchase **Synonyms**: client, buyer, account holder, user **Related Concepts**: CustomerAddress, SalesOrder **Examples**: - A new user registers and places their first order - An existing customer updates their shipping address ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── PRODUCT ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A tangible or digital item offered for sale on the platform. **Business Context**: Products are the core items that customers purchase. They are organized into categories and have inventory tracked across warehouses. **Key Attributes**: - Unique SKU code - Human-readable product name - Unit price in base currency (EUR) - Active/inactive status - Belongs to exactly one Category **Business Rules**: - Must belong to a valid category before being sold - Prices can change daily based on market conditions - Inactive products cannot be ordered but remain for historical reference **Synonyms**: item, article, goods, merchandise, SKU **Related Concepts**: Category, Inventory, OrderLineItem **Examples**: - "Wireless Bluetooth Headphones - Black" (SKU: ELE-001234) - "Cotton T-Shirt - Medium, Blue" (SKU: CLO-002345) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── SALES ORDER ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A formal transaction document recording the agreement between the platform and a Customer to supply Products. **Business Context**: Orders are created when customers complete checkout. They track the customer, items, payment status, and delivery information. **Key Attributes**: - Unique Order ID - References exactly one Customer - Order date when transaction was initiated - Total monetary value - Status: PENDING, CONFIRMED, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED - Must contain at least one Order Line Item **Business Rules**: - Cannot be shipped until payment is confirmed - Can only be cancelled if not yet shipped - Status changes are logged for audit purposes **Synonyms**: order, purchase order, transaction, customer order **Related Concepts**: Customer, OrderLineItem, CustomerAddress **Examples**: - Order #12345 for Customer #10001 containing 3 items - Order #12346 with status "DELIVERED" from last week ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ORDER LINE ITEM ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A single line within a Sales Order specifying a Product, quantity, and unit price at time of purchase. **Business Context**: Line items break down orders into individual product purchases. Each captures the price at the moment of ordering. **Key Attributes**: - Unique line identifier - References exactly one Product - Quantity of units ordered - Unit price at time of order (historical, not current) - Extended amount = quantity × unit price

SALES_ORDER_HDR.PAYMENT_CONFIRMED_AT (DATETIME, nullable) — Payment Confirmed At. Column in table SALES_ORDER_HDR: Stores the header information for sales orders, linking each order to a customer, recording the order date, total amount, status, and timestamps for payment confirmation, delivery, and creation.
Relationship: PAYMENT references SALES_ORDER_HDR via foreign key ORDER_ID -> SALES_ORDER_HDR.ORDER_ID.

PAYMENT.CONFIRMED_AT (DATETIME, nullable) — Confirmed At. Column in table PAYMENT: Stores records of payments associated with orders, including method, amount, status, and timestamps for when the payment was confirmed and created.

PAYMENT.STATUS_CODE (VARCHAR, nullable) — Status Code. Column in table PAYMENT: Stores records of payments associated with orders, including method, amount, status, and timestamps for when the payment was confirmed and created.

PAYMENT.AMOUNT (DECIMAL, NOT NULL) — Amount. Column in table PAYMENT: Stores records of payments associated with orders, including method, amount, status, and timestamps for when the payment was confirmed and created.

PAYMENT.ORDER_ID (BIGINT, NOT NULL) — Order ID. Column in table PAYMENT: Stores records of payments associated with orders, including method, amount, status, and timestamps for when the payment was confirmed and created.

Payment: Payment is the monetary transaction record associated with a Sales Order, identified by a unique PAYMENT_ID, containing the payment amount, and allowing one Sales Order to have one or more Payments.

per_question.question: How are shipments related to orders and warehouses?
per_question.expected_answer: Each shipment is for exactly one sales order (ORDER_ID in SHIPMENT references SALES_ORDER_HDR). The shipment also specifies the source warehouse code and includes tracking information and delivery status.
per_question.generated_answer:
Shipments are related to orders via `SHIPMENT.ORDER_ID` (BIGINT, `NOT NULL`), which is a foreign key to `SALES_ORDER_HDR(ORDER_ID)` (“Order being fulfilled”). The business rules also state that “a Shipment is for exactly one Sales Order,” while “one order may have multiple partial shipments,” so multiple `SHIPMENT` rows can reference the same `SALES_ORDER_HDR` order.

Shipments are related to warehouses through `SHIPMENT.WAREHOUSE_CODE`, described as the shipment’s “Source warehouse.” The column is nullable in the schema (`WAREHOUSE_CODE` is `YES`), and the business rules state that “a Shipment comes from exactly one Warehouse.”

per_question.question: Can a product belong to multiple categories?
per_question.expected_answer: No, each product belongs to exactly one category. The TB_PRODUCT table has a CATEGORY_ID foreign key referencing TB_CATEGORY, which stores a single category value per product.
per_question.generated_answer:
No. A product can belong to exactly one category.

The business concept for **PRODUCT** states it “**Belongs to exactly one Category**.” In the schema, **`TB_PRODUCT`** has a single **`CATEGORY_ID`** column marked **NOT NULL**, with a foreign key **`TB_PRODUCT.CATEGORY_ID → TB_CATEGORY(CATEGORY_ID)`**. There is no junction/mapping table shown that would allow a single product to reference multiple `TB_CATEGORY` rows.

per_question.question: Is it possible for a customer to place an order without payment?
per_question.expected_answer: Yes, an order can exist without payment. SALES_ORDER_HDR has STATUS_CODE defaulting to PENDING and PAYMENT_CONFIRMED_AT is nullable (NULL when no payment yet). The PAYMENT table links via ORDER_ID foreign key but nothing prevents an order from existing without a payment row. However, the business glossary states that an order cannot be shipped until payment is confirmed, so orders are created first and require payment confirmation before fulfillment.
per_question.generated_answer:
Yes.

At the schema level, **SALES_ORDER_HDR.PAYMENT_CONFIRMED_AT** is a **DATETIME that is nullable (YES)**, so an order can exist with **no confirmed payment timestamp**.

Also, the **PAYMENT** table references the order via **PAYMENT.ORDER_ID (NOT NULL) → SALES_ORDER_HDR(ORDER_ID)**. This means payment records (if they exist) must point to an existing order, but the provided schema details do not enforce that every sales order must already have a payment row.

What the rules do restrict is shipping: **“Cannot be shipped until payment is confirmed”** and **shipments can only be created for confirmed orders with payment.**

per_question.question: What schema fields support monetary value tracking across orders and their line items?
per_question.expected_answer: SALES_ORDER_HDR stores the header-level order amount in TOTAL_AMT (DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL). ORDER_LINE_ITEM provides the per-product breakdown: UNIT_PRICE (price locked at time of order, not updated with future changes), QUANTITY (constrained to > 0), and LINE_AMT (= QUANTITY × UNIT_PRICE). Both tables are linked via the ORDER_ID foreign key, enabling reconciliation between header totals and line-level amounts.

Monetary value tracking across sales orders and their line items is supported by these schema fields and their relationship.

At the order header level (**SALES_ORDER_HDR**), **`TOTAL_AMT (DECIMAL(12,2), NOT NULL)`** stores the order’s “Total order value” for each **`ORDER_ID`**.

At the line-item level (**ORDER_LINE_ITEM**), the amount math and stored line total are captured by:
- **`UNIT_PRICE (DECIMAL(10,2), NOT NULL)`**: unit price at the time of order
- **`QUANTITY (INT, NOT NULL)`**: number of units
- **`LINE_AMT (DECIMAL(12,2), NOT NULL)`**: extended amount, described as **quantity × unit price**

To roll up line-item value to the order, use the foreign key relationship **`ORDER_LINE_ITEM.ORDER_ID → SALES_ORDER_HDR.ORDER_ID`**, then aggregate **`SUM(ORDER_LINE_ITEM.LINE_AMT)`** by **`ORDER_ID`** and compare/reconcile to **`SALES_ORDER_HDR.TOTAL_AMT`**.

# Business Glossary - E-Commerce Domain (Simplified)

This document defines the core business concepts for the e-commerce platform test dataset.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CUSTOMER
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
**Definition**: An individual or entity registered on the platform who has made at least one purchase. **Business Context**: Customers are the primary actors in the e-commerce system. They browse products, place orders, and receive shipments. **Key Attributes**: - Unique Customer ID - Valid email address (required for login) - One or more delivery/billing addresses - Geographic region for tax calculation - Active/inactive status **Business Rules**: - Must place at least one order to remain active - Can have multiple addresses but only one primary per type - Data retained for 7 years after last purchase **Synonyms**: client, buyer, account holder, user **Related Concepts**: CustomerAddress, SalesOrder **Examples**: - A new user registers and places their first order - An existing customer updates their shipping address ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── PRODUCT ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A tangible or digital item offered for sale on the platform. **Business Context**: Products are the core items that customers purchase. They are organized into categories and have inventory tracked across warehouses. **Key Attributes**: - Unique SKU code - Human-readable product name - Unit price in base currency (EUR) - Active/inactive status - Belongs to exactly one Category **Business Rules**: - Must belong to a valid category before being sold - Prices can change daily based on market conditions - Inactive products cannot be ordered but remain for historical reference **Synonyms**: item, article, goods, merchandise, SKU **Related Concepts**: Category, Inventory, OrderLineItem **Examples**: - "Wireless Bluetooth Headphones - Black" (SKU: ELE-001234) - "Cotton T-Shirt - Medium, Blue" (SKU: CLO-002345) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── SALES ORDER ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A formal transaction document recording the agreement between the platform and a Customer to supply Products. **Business Context**: Orders are created when customers complete checkout. They track the customer, items, payment status, and delivery information. **Key Attributes**: - Unique Order ID - References exactly one Customer - Order date when transaction was initiated - Total monetary value - Status: PENDING, CONFIRMED, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED - Must contain at least one Order Line Item **Business Rules**: - Cannot be shipped until payment is confirmed - Can only be cancelled if not yet shipped - Status changes are logged for audit purposes **Synonyms**: order, purchase order, transaction, customer order **Related Concepts**: Customer, OrderLineItem, CustomerAddress **Examples**: - Order #12345 for Customer #10001 containing 3 items - Order #12346 with status "DELIVERED" from last week ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ORDER LINE ITEM ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: A single line within a Sales Order specifying a Product, quantity, and unit price at time of purchase. **Business Context**: Line items break down orders into individual product purchases. Each captures the price at the moment of ordering. **Key Attributes**: - Unique line identifier - References exactly one Product - Quantity of units ordered - Unit price at time of order (historical, not current) - Extended amount = quantity × unit price

**Business Context**: Line items break down orders into individual product purchases. Each captures the price at the moment of ordering. **Key Attributes**: - Unique line identifier - References exactly one Product - Quantity of units ordered - Unit price at time of order (historical, not current) - Extended amount = quantity × unit price **Business Rules**: - A Sales Order must contain at least one line item - Cannot be modified after order confirmation - Unit price is fixed and does not change with future price updates **Synonyms**: line item, order detail, order row **Related Concepts**: SalesOrder, Product **Examples**: - Line 1: 2 units of Wireless Headphones at €79.99 each - Line 2: 1 unit of USB Cable at €12.50 ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── PAYMENT ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: The monetary transaction that settles the total amount due for a Sales Order. **Business Context**: Payments authorize and transfer funds from the customer to the platform. Multiple payment methods are supported. **Key Attributes**: - Unique Payment ID - References exactly one Sales Order - Payment method (credit card, PayPal, bank transfer) - Payment amount - Confirmation timestamp - Status: PENDING, CONFIRMED, FAILED, REFUNDED **Business Rules**: - Payment must be confirmed before order ships - Refunds create new payment records with negative amounts - Multiple payment attempts can exist for one order **Synonyms**: transaction, payment transaction **Related Concepts**: SalesOrder **Examples**: - Credit card payment of €156.48 for Order #12345 - PayPal payment of €45.00 for Order #12346 ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── SHIPMENT ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── **Definition**: The physical delivery of Products from a Warehouse to a Customer's delivery address. **Business Context**: Shipments represent the logistics side of order fulfillment. They track what items were sent, when, and to whom. **Key Attributes**: - Unique Shipment ID - References exactly one Sales Order - Source Warehouse - Destination Customer Address - Shipped timestamp - Tracking number - Status: PREPARING, SHIPPED, IN_TRANSIT, DELIVERED **Business Rules**: - Can only be created for confirmed orders with payment - One order may have multiple partial shipments - Delivery address must belong to the order's customer **Synonyms**: delivery, package, consignment **Related Concepts**: SalesOrder, Warehouse, CustomerAddress, Inventory **Examples**: - Shipment #S-001 containing 2 items from Warehouse WH-EU-01 - Shipment #S-002 with tracking number "1Z999AA1" for Order #12347 ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── KEY RELATIONSHIP SUMMARY ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Customer Relationships: - A Customer places zero or more Sales Orders - A Customer has one or more Customer Addresses (not in simplified schema) Product Relationships: - A Product belongs to exactly one Category - A Product appears on zero or more Order Line Items Sales Order Relationships: - A Sales Order is placed by exactly one Customer - A Sales Order has one or more Order Line Items - A Sales Order has one or more Payments - A Sales Order has one or more Shipments Order Line Item Relationships: - An Order Line Item is part of exactly one Sales Order - An Order Line Item references exactly one Product Payment Relationships: - A Payment is for exactly one Sales Order

SHIPMENT.TRACKING_NUMBER (VARCHAR, nullable) — Tracking Number. Column in table SHIPMENT: Stores records of shipments associated with orders, including where and when items were shipped, tracking details, and current shipment status.

ORDER_LINE_ITEM.UNIT_PRICE (DECIMAL, NOT NULL) — Unit Price. Column in table ORDER_LINE_ITEM: Stores the line items for each order, including product, quantity, pricing, and the line-level total amount.
